title: Dependency Injection
description: Structural pattern in which a component’s dependencies are supplied by an external caller rather than instantiated internally. Enables substitution of implementations (real vs. stub) at call-site, improving testability and decoupling modules from their collaborators.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Dependency Injection is a pure code-level design pattern with no infrastructure or runtime service requirements. It involves structuring code to receive dependencies through constructors, setters, or interfaces rather than creating them internally. No additional servers, databases, cloud services, or SaaS subscriptions are required. The pattern affects code organization only, not operational costs.
provenance.adoptionCost: Low adoption cost for a well-established pattern. Most modern frameworks (Spring, .NET Core, NestJS, Laravel) have built-in DI containers. Implementation involves: (1) Refactoring constructors to accept interfaces (~2-4 hours for typical class), (2) Configuring DI container/bindings (~1-2 hours), (3) Writing interface definitions (~1-3 hours). With AI coding assistants, refactoring is largely automated. Team learning curve is minimal as DI is a fundamental pattern taught in most CS curricula. Estimated 8-12 developer hours at $25-40/hour blended rate = $200-$480. Testing improvements offset costs quickly through faster test execution and easier mocking.
provenance.licenseCost: Zero license cost. Dependency Injection is implemented using built-in language features (interfaces, constructors) and open-source DI containers/frameworks. Popular DI frameworks are free and open-source: Spring Framework (Apache 2.0), Autofac (.NET, MIT), Guice (Apache 2.0), Dagger (Apache 2.0), InversifyJS (MIT). No commercial licenses required. Some enterprise frameworks may bundle DI with broader tooling, but DI itself never requires licensing.

provides.reasoning: Dependency Injection enables easy substitution of real dependencies with mocks/stubs during testing, making unit tests more isolated and reliable.
By injecting dependencies externally rather than hard-coding them, components become decoupled from concrete implementations and depend only on abstractions/interfaces.
DI makes it easier to swap implementations and reconfigure components at runtime or deployment time without modifying client code.
External dependency injection creates well-defined component boundaries with explicit dependencies, enabling clearer module separation and reusability.
Explicit dependency declaration makes code easier to understand and modify, as all dependencies are visible at the injection point rather than hidden within implementation.

requires.reasoning: While basic DI can be implemented manually, complex scenarios with many dependencies benefit from an IoC container for lifecycle management and configuration.

supports_nfr.reason: Dependency Injection organises code behavior by wiring dependencies, not data content; it applies regardless of whether PII is present in the system.
Dependency Injection is a structural code pattern; GDPR compliance obligations apply to the system, not to this pattern specifically — it is applicable regardless of GDPR requirements.
Dependency Injection is a structural code pattern; CCPA compliance obligations apply to the system, not to this pattern specifically — it is applicable regardless of CCPA requirements.
Dependency Injection is a structural code pattern; HIPAA compliance obligations apply to the system, not to this pattern specifically — it is applicable regardless of HIPAA requirements.
Dependency Injection is a structural code pattern; SOX compliance obligations apply to the system, not to this pattern specifically — it is applicable regardless of SOX requirements.
Dependency Injection operates below the persistence layer, wiring components together; read-your-writes consistency is enforced at the data store, not by this pattern.
Dependency Injection does not implement persistence; strict durability guarantees are provided at the infrastructure layer and are orthogonal to this dependency-wiring pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Dependency Injection is a software design pattern that works on any cloud platform or on-premises.
Dependency Injection is applicable to all object-oriented and functional languages with first-class functions.
Dependency Injection is a code-level design pattern applicable to any platform or runtime environment.

injection_method.description: How dependencies are injected. Constructor injection (recommended) ensures immutability and required deps. Setter injection allows optional deps. Interface injection uses framework-specific interfaces

container_type.description: Dependency resolution approach. Manual wiring gives full control but verbose. Framework (Spring, Dagger, etc.) automates wiring with annotations. Service locator centralizes lookup but hides dependencies

lifecycle_management.description: Instance lifecycle strategy. Singleton creates one shared instance (memory efficient, stateless services). Transient creates new instance per request (stateful, isolated). Scoped ties to request/session context

interface_abstraction.description: Whether to inject interfaces vs concrete classes. True enables better testability with mocks and loose coupling. False is simpler but tightly couples to implementations
